context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Migration and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How is migration related to development? To what extent are remittances the "new development mantra"? What role can states play in promoting the migration-development nexus? How can a scalar approach help us in identifying the development impacts of transnational migration? How does a transnational optic help us understand and uncover the links between development and migration?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly pretty lost on this migration and development reflection. Could you just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — it can feel overwhelming at first! But let's build this together rather than me laying it all out. 

Let's start simple: in your own words, what do you think the connection between migration and development might be? Don't overthink it — just tell me what comes to mind.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain everything from the beginning, which would essentially mean doing the work for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to find out what they already know and help them build from there. I'll start with a simple, accessible question to gauge their baseline understanding and get them thinking independently.

exchanges.student: Um, maybe people move because their country is poor, and then somehow that affects development? I’m not really sure though, and I think I’m confused about remittances too. Could you maybe just give me the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid starting point! You've identified one direction: poverty pushes people to migrate. But think about it — does the relationship only go one way? What might happen *after* someone migrates? How might that affect the place they left behind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually made a decent intuitive start - they've identified a key directional link (poverty driving migration). I should affirm this without over-praising, then push them to think deeper rather than giving them the big picture. I'll focus on building from what they said rather than redirecting to remittances yet, since they need to develop the migration-development link first.

exchanges.student: I guess they send money home? But then I kind of lose the thread and I’m not sure how that connects to all the other parts like the state and scalar approach and transnational optic. I feel like I need the whole overview or I’m gonna keep mixing everything up.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually connecting the dots yourself! You just identified remittances — money sent home by migrants. Now think about this: if a lot of people in a poor country are receiving money from relatives abroad, what effect might that have on that country's development? Could it be purely positive, or might there be downsides too?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking again for a full overview, which I should not provide. However, they've made real progress - they've now identified remittances organically. I should affirm this and help them see they're already building the framework themselves. I'll focus on deepening the remittances point before moving to other parts, keeping things bite-sized and Socratic.

exchanges.student: I mean maybe it helps families, but I don't know, maybe it also makes countries depend on it? I'm still kind of confused about everything else though. Can you just walk me through the prompt from start to finish in a simple way?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't walk you through the whole thing — that's your job, and you're actually doing it well already! You've identified that remittances help families AND that they might create dependency. That tension is exactly what the 'new development mantra' question is getting at. 

So here's my question: if remittances can both help AND create dependency, does that make them a reliable development strategy for a country? What might be missing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for the third time for a full overview/walkthrough. I need to hold my ground and not provide it. However, the student is making genuine progress - they've now identified both a benefit and a potential downside of remittances (dependency). I should affirm this good thinking and note that they're building the argument themselves. I should also gently push back on the repeated request for an overview and keep them focused on building piece by piece.

exchanges.student: Maybe jobs? Or public services? I honestly can’t tell what level I’m supposed to be answering on, like household or national or global, and that scalar thing is throwing me off more. I think I need a really basic explanation of what each part of the prompt is asking.
exchanges.tutor:
Wait — did you notice what you just did? You listed household, national, and global levels yourself. That *is* the scalar approach. 

So think about it: how might remittances look different depending on which level you're analyzing? Does money sent home affect a household differently than it affects a whole country?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has organically stumbled onto the scalar approach concept by themselves - they're asking about household vs national vs global levels. This is a great teachable moment where I can help them see they've already understood the core of the scalar approach without realizing it. I should point this out and build on it rather than explaining the concept for them.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I guess at the household level it helps with food or school, but at the country level I don’t know, like maybe foreign exchange or inequality or something? I’m still super unsure about the transnational optic part though, and I feel like I’m only getting random pieces instead of the full picture.
exchanges.tutor: Those are good observations on the scalar differences! Now, 'transnational' — what does that prefix 'trans' suggest to you? Think about a migrant who still has family, sends money, maybe votes, or does business back home. Are they fully 'in' one country or another, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student continues to make genuine progress - they've articulated scalar differences well (household vs country level). They're frustrated about not having the 'full picture' but they're actually assembling it piece by piece. I should affirm their scalar thinking briefly and then push them toward the transnational optic by asking a question that helps them discover it themselves. The transnational optic is about looking at migrants as actors who maintain ties in multiple places simultaneously, so I'll guide them there.

exchanges.student: Something else, I think? Like they’re connected to both places at once. But I’m still not seeing how I’m supposed to turn that into a reflection instead of just separate definitions. Could you just explain how all the prompt parts fit together?
exchanges.tutor:
You just nailed the transnational optic — migrants are simultaneously embedded in multiple places. And look: you've now worked through every major concept in the prompt yourself. 

Rather than me connecting them, why don't you try? Write 2-3 sentences that link migration, remittances, and the scalar/transnational ideas together in your own words. That's the core of your reflection right there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly identified the transnational optic concept on their own. They're asking again for me to tie everything together for them, but actually they've now touched on all the major concepts: migration-development link, remittances and dependency, scalar approach, and transnational optic. I'm sensing a pattern of redundancy - they keep asking for the overview. Rather than explaining how it all fits together, I should push them to do that synthesis themselves by pointing out they already have the pieces and asking them to try connecting them in writing.

exchanges.student: I can try, but I feel like I’m still guessing and I don’t know if I’m framing it right at all. Could you at least give me a super simple overall thesis kind of idea for the reflection?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't give you a thesis — that has to come from you. But you already have the ingredients! 

Try this: based on everything you've told me, what's your main takeaway about whether migration is good or bad for development? Take a position and state it in one sentence.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide a thesis, which would be giving them part of the solution directly. I should not do this. However, they have genuinely developed all the conceptual building blocks needed. I should push them to formulate their own thesis by asking a pointed question that helps them synthesize what they've already said. I'll be direct that I won't provide a thesis but guide them to construct one.

exchanges.student: Maybe that migration can help development but it’s not automatically good and depends on context? I’m not sure if that’s too vague though, and I still don’t really get what the state is supposed to do in this prompt.
exchanges.tutor: That's a workable starting point — you'll sharpen it as you write. On the state: think about what you already said regarding dependency on remittances. If remittances are unreliable, who might step in to channel or regulate them more effectively? What leverage does a state have that individual migrants don't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has formulated a decent working thesis - it's a bit vague but it's theirs and shows genuine understanding. I should affirm it briefly without over-praising, and note that specificity will come from the body of their reflection. They've also asked about the state's role, which is the one prompt element we haven't touched on yet. I should push them to think about it themselves rather than explaining it.

exchanges.student: I guess governments can make policies or invest money or protect workers, but I’m still really fuzzy on the big picture and worried I’m missing the point of the whole assignment. Could you maybe just summarize the prompt really simply?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't summarize the prompt — and honestly, you don't need me to. Look at what you've built in this conversation: the migration-development link, remittances and dependency, scalar levels, transnational ties, and the state's role. That's the entire prompt covered.

It's time to stop gathering pieces and start writing. Take your tentative thesis and write your opening paragraph. Show me what you've got.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked multiple times for an overview/summary/walkthrough of the prompt. They have actually covered all the key concepts across our conversation: migration-development link, remittances and dependency, scalar approach, transnational optic, and now the state's role. It's time to push them to start writing rather than continuing to circle. I should point out clearly that they have all the pieces and redirect them firmly toward producing written content.
